items.title: Read 'Agent-as-a-Judge' (paper of the day)
items.desc: 20-min skim of arXiv 2410.10934. Maps step-by-step evaluation onto MAE's 29-evaluator suite — directly answers a question Jun raised in onboarding: do we judge final outputs or every agent step? Take notes you can quote in week-2 standup.

sections.title: 🎯 Today's single side-project focus (D009)
sections.text: **morning-brief** — drain at least 1 of R006–R009 so night-builder has a target. **Crumbs, BedRock, Gusto, learn-everything frozen** until a decision flips. MAE (day job) runs in parallel and is unaffected.

sections.title: 📄 Paper of the day — Evaluate agents step-by-step, not just outcome

paper.title: Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents
paper.authors: Mingchen Zhuge, Changsheng Zhao, Dylan Ashley, et al. (Meta AI + KAUST)
paper.tldr: Existing eval is binary (final-outcome only) or expensive (full human review). Agent-as-a-Judge runs an evaluator-agent alongside the agent under test, scoring intermediate steps and producing reward signals at ~3% the cost of human eval with comparable agreement.
paper.why: Direct fit for MAE: your 29 evaluators today mostly judge final artefacts. The step-wise critique pattern is a natural upgrade for the 10 LLM-judge slots — cheaper than human PPE runs, and produces calibration signal you can backprop into prompts. Worth quoting to Jun this week.
key_findings: DevAI benchmark: 55 realistic dev tasks with hierarchical user requirements — agents must satisfy a tree of sub-goals, not a single answer.
Agent-judge agreement with human raters approaches LLM-as-judge agreement at far lower cost; intermediate critique catches failures that pass end-to-end checks.
Reward signal from the judge can be reused to improve the agent under test (closing the eval-driven-dev loop Jun is pushing for).

sections.title: 🌐 What the world is reading (HN front page)

items.title: Amateur armed with ChatGPT solves an Erdős problem
items.summary: A non-mathematician used ChatGPT to crack a 60-year-old Erdős combinatorics problem, with the model proposing a candidate construction the human then verified.
items.why: Concrete instance of agentic AI as a research multiplier — exactly the workflow you're betting on for MAE eval-driven dev. Worth one paragraph in your weekly synthesis.

items.title: The West forgot how to make things, now it's forgetting how to code
items.summary: Argues the same de-skilling that hollowed Western manufacturing is now hitting software as juniors lean on LLMs without building underlying intuition.
items.why: Direct counter-frame to your eval-driven-dev thesis: if humans hand off too much to agents without judges, capability rots. Strengthens the case for step-wise evaluators.

items.title: Show HN: Turning a Gaussian Splat into a videogame
items.summary: Walks through importing a Gaussian Splat into a real-time engine, swapping baked lighting, and turning a captured scene into an interactive level.
items.why: Sits at AI rendering × video gen — adjacent to your differentiable-rendering interests. Bookmark for the rendering-side notebook.
